thread.title: Shapiro diverts federal funds to SEPTA to prevent fare increase, service cuts
thread.title_full: Shapiro diverts federal funds to SEPTA to prevent fare increase, service cuts

title: Shapiro diverts federal funds to SEPTA to prevent fare increase, service cuts
text:
ZIP Advertisement Pennsylvania governor diverts federal funds to SEPTA to prevent fare increase, service cuts Share Copy Link Copy {copyShortcut} to copy Link copied! Updated: 5:55 PM EST Nov 22, 2024 Pennsylvania governor diverts federal funds to SEPTA to prevent fare increase, service cuts Share Copy Link Copy {copyShortcut} to copy Link copied! Updated: 5:55 PM EST Nov 22, 2024 REMAINS UNDER INVESTIGATION. THERE IS NO REASON TO KEEP THAT MONEY ON OUR BALANCE SHEET IN THE STATE WHEN WE CAN INVEST IT IN SEPTA, RIGHT NOW AND HELP THIS COMMUNITY. NEW AT 530, PENNSYLVANIA GOVERNOR JOSH SHAPIRO HAS DIVERTED MORE THAN $150 MILLION OF FEDERAL HIGHWAY CAPITAL FUNDS TO SEPTA TO KEEP FARES DOWN ON THE TRANSIT SYSTEM. SEPTA PREVIOUSLY ANNOUNCED IT WOULD INCREASE FARES BY 29% AND REDUCE SERVICES BY 20%, BEGINNING IN THE NEW YEAR. OFFICIALS SAY THEY WERE FORCED TO IMPLEMENT THE NEW PLAN AFTER LAWMAKERS SHOT DOWN A STATE BUDGET THAT WOULD HAVE GIVEN THE SYSTEM $161 MILLION, $153 MILLION WILL BE FLEXED FROM PENNDOT’S BUDGET EFFECTIVE IMMEDIATELY TO KEEP SEPTA’S CRITICAL OPERATIONS GOING UNTIL AT LEAST JULY 2025. SHAPIRO SAYS HIS ADMINISTRATION IS PUTTING INFRASTRUCTURE AT THE FOREFRONT, EVEN MENTIONING A PROJECT IN THE SUSQUEHANNA VALLEY. I WAS STANDING OUT IN MANHEIM TOWNSHIP, OUT IN RURAL LANCASTER COUNTY. I WAS LISTENING TO FOLKS IN THAT COMMUNITY TALK ABOUT HOW, BECAUSE WE CAME TOGETHER TO FIX THE BRIDGE OVER RIFE RUN, THAT THEY WERE ABLE TO GET TO WORK QUICKER, JUST LIKE WE REPAIR AND MAINTAIN OUR ROADS AND BRIDGES IN THOSE RURAL AND IN THOSE SUBURBAN COMMUNITIES. I THINK WE OWE IT TO THE GOOD PEOPLE OF PENNSYLVANIA WHO TAKE MASS TRANSIT TO BE THERE FOR THEM AND THEIR FAMILIES AS WELL. SHAPIRO MADE THE FUNDING ANNOUNCEMENT TODAY ALONGSIDE LOCAL LAWMAKERS IN PHILADELPHIA, SAYING IT WILL SAVE COMMUTERS MONEY, BUT REPUBLICANS ARE ALREADY PUSHING BACK. PENNSYLVANIA SENATE PRESIDENT PRO TEM KIM WARD, A REPUBLICAN, SAID THAT THE FUNDING CRISIS SEPTA IS FACING IS A CRISIS OF SHAPIRO’S MAKING, SAYING IN A STATEMENT THAT THIS MOVE IS AN EXAMPLE OF HOW SIMPLY SHOWING UP FALLS SHORT IN ADDRESSING COMPLEX ISSUES AND LOCALLY, REPUBLICAN STATE REPRESENTATIVE SETH GROVE OF YORK COUNTY SAYS THIS DECISION HARMS DRIVERS AND TAKES AWAY FUNDING FOR PROJECTS IN THE SUSQUEHANNA VALLEY. SHAPIRO CLARIFIED THAT THOSE SEVEN PROJECTS PENNDOT IS FLEXING THE MONEY FROM, WHICH INCLUDES A PLAN TO REPAVE PART GET LOCAL BREAKING NEWS ALERTS The latest breaking updates, delivered straight to your email inbox.
Your Email Address Submit Privacy Notice Advertisement Pennsylvania governor diverts federal funds to SEPTA to prevent fare increase, service cuts Share Copy Link Copy {copyShortcut} to copy Link copied! Updated: 5:55 PM EST Nov 22, 2024 Pennsylvania Gov. Josh Shapiro is diverting $153 million of federal highway capital funds to the Southeastern Pennsylvania Transportation Authority to prevent service cuts and a fare increase.SEPTA, which serves 800,000 people daily, is facing a shortfall due to the expiration of federal pandemic relief funds.During a news conference in Philadelphia on Friday, Shapiro said shifting the money will allow SEPTA to maintain critical operations through at least July 2025.The governor said his administration is putting infrastructure at the forefront – even mentioning a project in the Susquehanna Valley."I was standing out in Manheim Township, out in rural Lancaster County. I was listening to folks in that community talk about how because we came together to fix the bridge over Rife Run, that they were able to get to work quicker. Just like we repair and maintain our roads and bridges in those rural and suburban communities, I think we owe it to the good people of Pennsylvania who take mass transit to be there for them and their families as well," he said.The administration said the flexing of federal highway capital funds is a standard practice in Pennsylvania and across the nation. It temporarily reallocates funds from projects that aren't underway yet or have been put out to bid.Moving the funds doesn't halt any ongoing infrastructure projects already underway across the Commonwealth, according to the administration.Republicans push backRepublicans are criticizing the governor's action.Some state lawmakers released statements after the announcement.Senate President Pro Tempore Kim Ward"Today's announcement by Gov. Josh Shapiro to address the mass transit crisis facing SEPTA is a crisis of his own making. It's yet another example of how simply ‘showing up’ falls short in addressing complex issues. Gov. Shapiro and House Democrats prioritized education over mass transit by approving the largest budget increase ever for a traditional education system that continues to trap Philadelphia's kids in failing schools. The bottom line is this: Pennsylvanians – especially those in the southeast – are losing out, not because of divided government, but because Pennsylvania Democrats have chosen to focus their priorities and spending on one area alone."Rep. Seth Grove, R-Dover"The unilateral decision by the governor to slash funding for Pennsylvania’s transportation needs by redirecting it to Philadelphia’s public transportation authority, SEPTA, harms drivers, including those in York County. As its name implies, Federal Highway Funding is meant to fix and improve roads and highways, not to prop up public transport."Shapiro's order to direct money away from fixing roads will only exacerbate the problem of deteriorating roads and bridges by further delaying when they can be fixed. It means Pennsylvania drivers will have to continue to deal with additional wear and tear on their vehicles. All told, seven projects across the state that were to be funded in 2025 will be delayed. That includes a project to resurface part of Interstate 83 in York County."Additionally, mass transit benefits local areas; and counties that use SEPTA should be tasked with filling the budget gap. State government already overfunds SEPTA."Sen. Scott Martin, R-Lancaster/Berks"As I mentioned in a press conference two weeks ago, SEPTA must show a commitment to needed reforms and address fares that haven’t been changed since 2014. Instead, the Governor bailed them out by putting a Band-Aid on a wound much bigger, and diverted money away from Pennsylvania's other critical infrastructure needs – almost all of which are in districts represented by Republicans."We've been clear that transit isn't the only part of our transportation network. The solutions we develop should benefit ALL Pennsylvania's transportation infrastructure needs."It's also important to point out that this is a one-time fix, not a sustainable model. Without critical steps to right-size SEPTA and make it safer, taxpayers will continue to foot the bill for hundreds of millions of dollars in added costs every year. As hardworking Pennsylvanians struggle to make ends meet, the Governor and House Democrats continue to take actions that will worsen the state’s structural deficit and march us towards higher taxes on families. The Shapiro shovel just keeps making the hole deeper." PHILADELPHIA — Pennsylvania Gov. Josh Shapiro is diverting $153 million of federal highway capital funds to the Southeastern Pennsylvania Transportation Authority to prevent service cuts and a fare increase.
SEPTA, which serves 800,000 people daily, is facing a shortfall due to the expiration of federal pandemic relief funds.
Advertisement During a news conference in Philadelphia on Friday, Shapiro said shifting the money will allow SEPTA to maintain critical operations through at least July 2025.
The governor said his administration is putting infrastructure at the forefront – even mentioning a project in the Susquehanna Valley.
"I was standing out in Manheim Township, out in rural Lancaster County. I was listening to folks in that community talk about how because we came together to fix the bridge over Rife Run, that they were able to get to work quicker. Just like we repair and maintain our roads and bridges in those rural and suburban communities, I think we owe it to the good people of Pennsylvania who take mass transit to be there for them and their families as well," he said.
The administration said the flexing of federal highway capital funds is a standard practice in Pennsylvania and across the nation. It temporarily reallocates funds from projects that aren't underway yet or have been put out to bid.
Moving the funds doesn't halt any ongoing infrastructure projects already underway across the Commonwealth, according to the administration.
Republicans push back Republicans are criticizing the governor's action.
Some state lawmakers released statements after the announcement.
Senate President Pro Tempore Kim Ward
"Today's announcement by Gov. Josh Shapiro to address the mass transit crisis facing SEPTA is a crisis of his own making. It's yet another example of how simply ‘showing up’ falls short in addressing complex issues. Gov. Shapiro and House Democrats prioritized education over mass transit by approving the largest budget increase ever for a traditional education system that continues to trap Philadelphia's kids in failing schools. The bottom line is this: Pennsylvanians – especially those in the southeast – are losing out, not because of divided government, but because Pennsylvania Democrats have chosen to focus their priorities and spending on one area alone."
Rep. Seth Grove, R-Dover
"The unilateral decision by the governor to slash funding for Pennsylvania’s transportation needs by redirecting it to Philadelphia’s public transportation authority, SEPTA, harms drivers, including those in York County. As its name implies, Federal Highway Funding is meant to fix and improve roads and highways, not to prop up public transport.
"Shapiro's order to direct money away from fixing roads will only exacerbate the problem of deteriorating roads and bridges by further delaying when they can be fixed. It means Pennsylvania drivers will have to continue to deal with additional wear and tear on their vehicles. All told, seven projects across the state that were to be funded in 2025 will be delayed. That includes a project to resurface part of Interstate 83 in York County.
"Additionally, mass transit benefits local areas; and counties that use SEPTA should be tasked with filling the budget gap. State government already overfunds SEPTA."
Sen. Scott Martin, R-Lancaster/Berks
"As I mentioned in a press conference two weeks ago , SEPTA must show a commitment to needed reforms and address fares that haven’t been changed since 2014. Instead, the Governor bailed them out by putting a Band-Aid on a wound much bigger, and diverted money away from Pennsylvania's other critical infrastructure needs – almost all of which are in districts represented by Republicans.
"We've been clear that transit isn't the only part of our transportation network. The solutions we develop should benefit ALL Pennsylvania's transportation infrastructure needs.
"It's also important to point out that this is a one-time fix, not a sustainable model. Without critical steps to right-size SEPTA and make it safer, taxpayers will continue to foot the bill for hundreds of millions of dollars in added costs every year. As hardworking Pennsylvanians struggle to make ends meet, the Governor and House Democrats continue to take actions that will worsen the state’s structural deficit and march us towards higher taxes on families. The Shapiro shovel just keeps making the hole deeper."
